input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **physical twin system** is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** designed for research and educational purposes. It consists of:
  - **Physical components**: A gantry crane structure with mechanical actuators (likely linear/rotary motors) for movement, integrated with a **dedicated controller** for real-time operation.
  - **Operational domain**: A **controlled lab environment**, enabling precise experimentation, validation, and bidirectional data exchange with its digital counterpart.
  - **Key characteristics**:
    - **Modularity**: Suitable for integration with various digital twin services (e.g., control, monitoring, validation).
    - **Reproducibility**: Open-source and publicly available, relying on **freely accessible software** to ensure accessibility for research and education.
    - **Bidirectional interaction**: Supports **real-time data streaming** from the physical system to the digital twin and vice versa, enabling closed-loop validation and optimization.
    - **Scalability**: Designed as a **demonstrator** for digital twin research, including studies on **continuous validation methods**, **fault detection**, and **system evolution notations** (e.g., DarTwin).
    - **Mechanical/kinematic focus**: Incorporates **CAD models** and a **kinematic model** to simulate motion dynamics and structural behavior.
  The system is explicitly cited as a **use case for validating digital twin functionalities** in prior publications, emphasizing its role as a **low-cost, reproducible exemplar** for cyber-physical system research.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin** provides the following **specialized services**, categorized by type and capability:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - **Historical data logging**: Captures operational data (e.g., actuator positions, sensor readings) for post-analysis and trend evaluation.
    - **Real-time telemetry**: Streams sensor/actuator data from the physical crane to the digital twin for immediate processing.
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Optimal control services**: Implements algorithms to optimize crane movement, energy consumption, or operational efficiency based on real-time or historical data.
  - **Validation**:
    - **Continuous validation methods**: Uses **model-based validation** (e.g., comparing kinematic predictions with physical sensor data) to ensure consistency between the digital and physical twins. Validated in prior works ([4], [5]).
    - **Fault detection**: Employs **anomaly detection techniques** to identify deviations in crane behavior (e.g., actuator failures, structural stress) by cross-referencing digital twin simulations with physical telemetry ([6]).
  - **Visualization**:
    - **Interactive 3D/2D dashboards**: Renders **CAD-based visualizations** of the crane’s state (e.g., real-time position, trajectory) for operators or researchers.
    - **Data-driven insights**: Provides **visual analytics** (e.g., heatmaps, time-series plots) to correlate physical performance with digital twin predictions.
  - **System Evolution**:
    - **DarTwin notation support**: Facilitates **documentation of system changes** (e.g., hardware upgrades, control algorithm updates) via a standardized notation for tracking digital twin evolution ([7]).
  - **Target users**:
    - **Researchers**: For validating digital twin methodologies (e.g., synchronization, fault detection).
    - **Educators**: As a **teaching tool** for cyber-physical systems, control theory, and digital twin principles.
    - **Operators**: For **real-time monitoring** and **predictive maintenance** insights.
  The services are **bidirectionally integrated** with the physical system, enabling **closed-loop improvements** (e.g., adjusting control parameters dynamically based on twin-derived insights).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin** is implemented using the following **tools, technologies, and enablers**, with their specific roles:
  - **Software Platforms/Frameworks**:
    - **Open-source ecosystem**: Relies exclusively on **freely available tools** to ensure reproducibility (no proprietary dependencies).
    - **Simulation Engines**:
      - **CAD tools**: Likely **FreeCAD**, **Blender**, or similar for **3D modeling** of the gantry crane (exact tool not specified but implied by 'CAD models' mention).
      - **Kinematic simulation**: Uses **open-source kinematics libraries** (e.g., **Python-based libraries like `pybullet` or `pykinematics`**) to model joint trajectories and structural dynamics.
    - **Data Processing**:
      - **Historical logging**: Implemented via **lightweight databases** (e.g., **SQLite**, **InfluxDB**) or **time-series databases** for storing sensor/actuator data.
      - **Real-time streaming**: Leverages **MQTT** or **ROS (Robot Operating System)** for **low-latency data exchange** between the physical crane and digital twin.
    - **Control Systems**:
      - **Embedded controllers**: The physical crane uses a **dedicated microcontroller** (e.g., **Arduino**, **Raspberry Pi**) or **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** for real-time actuation.
      - **Digital twin control services**: Implements **optimal control algorithms** (e.g., **PID tuning**, **model predictive control**) via **Python (SciPy, NumPy)** or **MATLAB/Simulink** (if used for prototyping).
    - **Visualization**:
      - **Web-based dashboards**: Built with **JavaScript frameworks** (e.g., **D3.js**, **Plotly**) or **Python libraries** (e.g., **Dash**, **Bokeh**) for interactive visualization.
      - **3D rendering**: Uses **WebGL** or **Three.js** for real-time visualization of the crane’s digital model.
    - **Validation Tools**:
      - **Model comparison**: Employs **statistical validation methods** (e.g., **residual analysis**, **confidence intervals**) to quantify discrepancies between digital and physical twins.
      - **Fault detection**: Implements **machine learning models** (e.g., **isolated forest**, **autoencoders**) or **rule-based systems** for anomaly detection.
    - **System Evolution**:
      - **DarTwin notation**: A **custom or domain-specific notation** (not specified further) for documenting system changes, likely integrated into a **version control system** (e.g., **Git**) or **digital twin management platform**.
    - **Development Tools**:
      - **Programming languages**: Primarily **Python** (for data processing, visualization, and control) due to its open-source ecosystem and compatibility with digital twin tools.
      - **Version control**: **Git** for tracking code and model changes across iterations.
    - **Key Enablers**:
      - **Bidirectional communication**: Achieved via **REST APIs**, **WebSockets**, or **ROS topics** for seamless data flow between the physical and digital domains.
      - **Modular architecture**: Enables **plug-and-play integration** of new services (e.g., adding a new validation module without redesigning the entire twin).
      - **Reproducibility**: The entire setup is **documented and shared openly**, with dependencies on **standardized tools** (e.g., ROS for robotics, SQLite for databases) to minimize setup barriers.
  The exemplar avoids proprietary tools to ensure **long-term accessibility** and **community adoption**, aligning with the goal of fostering **open and reproducible digital twin research**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin systems described in the documents exhibit a decentralized, ad-hoc architecture with no explicit mention of multiple Digital Twin instances. The GreenhouseDT exemplar relies on a single Digital Twin instance synchronized with the physical greenhouse via an InfluxDB time-series database, which implies periodic data synchronization (though specific frequencies are not detailed). The Air Quality Management exemplar uses a Raspberry Pi with sensors for the physical system and an AutomationML-based Digital Twin, suggesting a one-to-one mapping between the physical and digital components without hierarchical multiplicities. The Lab Scale Gantry Crane system is not detailed in the provided documents regarding multiplicities or hierarchical structures, but the context diagram implies a centralized control architecture with no explicit decentralized Digital Twin instances. No coordination mechanisms between twins are mentioned, and the scope of responsibilities remains unclear for any potential twin instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin systems in the documents employ an ad-hoc orchestration approach rather than a commercial framework. The GreenhouseDT system uses **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage, **ActiveMQ** for message brokering, and **virtual machines** with pre-recorded data to facilitate synchronization between the physical greenhouse and its Digital Twin. The Air Quality Management exemplar integrates **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation and relies on **AutomationML** for Digital Twin representation, with a Raspberry Pi acting as the physical sensor node. The Lab Scale Gantry Crane system is described using the **C4 Model** for architecture visualization, implying a structured but unspecified orchestration pattern. Component integration is achieved via containerization/virtualization (e.g., virtual machines) and message brokers (e.g., ActiveMQ), while resource management is not explicitly detailed. System-wide coordination is implicit through these technologies but lacks formalized patterns or frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin systems exhibit limited horizontal integration with external systems. The GreenhouseDT exemplar integrates **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) for internal communication, with no explicit mention of interoperability standards or dependencies on external enterprise systems. The Air Quality Management exemplar uses **Microsoft Azure tools**, which suggests potential integration with cloud-based services (e.g., Azure IoT Hub, Azure Functions) but does not detail specific protocols or standards. The Raspberry Pi-based sensor system in this exemplar relies on AutomationML, a standardized format for digital twins, which could facilitate interoperability with other AutomationML-compatible systems. The Lab Scale Gantry Crane system is not described in terms of external integration, and no data exchange protocols or interoperability standards are mentioned. Overall, integration patterns are ad-hoc and limited to internal components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system for the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs a self-adaptive digital twin architecture with behavioral adaptation for both the physical greenhouse and its digital counterpart. It integrates **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage, enabling efficient management of environmental sensor data (e.g., temperature, humidity, light). The digital twin pipeline relies on **ActiveMQ** as a message broker for real-time communication between components, facilitating event-driven data exchange. The system includes **prerecorded data** within virtual machine images for ease of deployment, though no explicit mention of model types (geometric, behavioral, analytical) is provided. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling, focusing on indoor air quality simulation and prediction, with Microsoft Azure tools supporting the pipeline. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not explicitly detail its computational models or data components in the provided documents, but the C4 Model suggests a software-centric architecture with potential integration of simulation models for crane dynamics and container logistics. No specific model relationships or data management approaches (e.g., federated learning, edge computing) are mentioned beyond containerization/virtualization technologies for dissemination.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar utilizes a physical sensing infrastructure within the greenhouse, though specific sensor types, spatial distribution, or sampling frequencies are not detailed. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar explicitly employs a **Raspberry Pi** with a **set of sensors** for real-time indoor air quality monitoring, likely including CO₂, particulate matter, and temperature/humidity sensors. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not provide explicit details about its sensing components in the provided documents, though the context diagram (Fig. 2) implies sensors for position tracking (e.g., encoders, LiDAR) and environmental monitoring (e.g., load cells) for container logistics. No accuracy/precision specifications or data transmission rates are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral models** for both physical and digital systems, implying dynamic validation mechanisms to ensure alignment between the twin and the greenhouse. However, no explicit methods for model fidelity quantification (e.g., RMSE, correlation analysis) or uncertainty sources (e.g., sensor noise, environmental variability) are described. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction**, suggesting validation via comparative analysis against real-time sensor data, though no formal verification methods (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations) are mentioned. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not detail fidelity/validity considerations in the provided documents, though the C4 Model implies architectural validation through software testing frameworks. No quality assurance processes (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) or error correction mechanisms are referenced.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system involves continuous data acquisition from the **Physical Crane** hardware to the digital components via the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program). Key data flows include:
- **Data Types Transmitted**: Sensor measurements (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration) and execution state logs of crane movements.
- **Communication Protocols**: Direct communication between the **Physical Crane Interface** and crane controllers (exact protocol unspecified but implied to be hardware-specific).
- **Transmission Frequencies**: Continuous logging by the **Logging Application**, though specific frequency not detailed; assumed to be high enough for real-time monitoring (e.g., sub-second intervals).
- **Event Triggers**: No explicit event-based triggers mentioned; data is logged continuously via the **Logging Application**.
- **Data Quality Assurance**: No explicit mention of validation or filtering steps, but data is stored in a **TimescaleDB** database, which inherently supports time-series data integrity.
- **Preprocessing Steps**: Not explicitly described; data is directly ingested into the database for downstream services.
- **Data Integration Mechanisms**: The **MQTT Broker** facilitates signaling between services, ensuring synchronized data flow to the **Dashboard**, **Trajectory Generator Service**, and **Simulation Service**. The **Continuous Validation Service** compares real-time measurements with simulated trajectories, though this is a post-processing validation step rather than preprocessing.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this system is mediated by the **Control Application**, which generates and sends control commands to the **Physical Crane Interface**. Key components include:
- **Command Types**: Optimal movement trajectories generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service**, designed to eliminate residual swinging motion at the end of movements.
- **Control Message Formats**: Commands are transmitted via the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python-based) to the crane controllers; exact format unspecified but inferred to align with the crane’s hardware API.
- **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**: No explicit authentication mechanisms mentioned; validation occurs post-execution via the **Continuous Validation Service**, which compares real trajectories (from sensors) with simulated trajectories (from the **Simulation Service**). If discrepancies exceed acceptable thresholds, operators are notified via the **Dashboard**.
- **Feedback Systems**: The **Continuous Validation Service** acts as a feedback loop by comparing real-time sensor data with simulated predictions, enabling adaptive adjustments.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Not explicitly detailed; assumed to rely on operator intervention via the **Human Machine Interface** if the **Continuous Validation Service** flags anomalies.
- **Command Execution Confirmation**: No explicit confirmation mechanism described; reliance on the **Logging Application** for post-execution state logging.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** infrastructure is centered around an **MQTT Broker** for inter-service communication, with the following specifics:
- **Communication Protocols**: MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) for signaling between containers (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, **Continuous Validation Service**).
- **Network Topology and Architecture**: Not explicitly detailed, but implied to be a centralized architecture with the **MQTT Broker** as the hub for service communication. The **Physical Crane Interface** connects directly to the crane hardware.
- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**: Not quantified; assumed to be low-latency given the real-time nature of crane control and validation (e.g., sub-second response times for trajectory adjustments).
- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**: No redundancy mechanisms mentioned; reliance on the **Continuous Validation Service** for anomaly detection and operator intervention.
- **Security Protocols**: Not explicitly mentioned; assumed to be minimal or none, given the lab-scale context and lack of explicit security references.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is described as follows:
- **Hosting Platforms**: Not explicitly specified; implied to be **on-premise** given the lab-scale context and lack of cloud/edge references.
- **Computational Requirements**: The **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, and **Continuous Validation Service** require computational resources for kinematics modeling, simulation, and distance metric calculations (e.g., trajectory comparison). No specific hardware specs provided.
- **Storage Requirements**: Data is stored in a **TimescaleDB** (time-series database), which is optimized for high-volume, time-stamped data (e.g., crane sensor logs, trajectory records).
- **Scalability Mechanisms**: Not explicitly detailed; assumed to be limited by the lab-scale scope and lack of distributed deployment references.
- **Deployment Models Supported**: No explicit mention; implied to be a monolithic or loosely coupled deployment (via MQTT) on a single machine or local network.
- **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: No redundancy or failover mechanisms described; reliance on operator monitoring via the **Dashboard** and manual intervention for anomalies.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
- **GreenhouseDT**:
      - **Methodologies**: Ad-hoc approach with behavioral adaptation of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin.
      - **Technologies**: Uses InfluxDB (time-series database), ActiveMQ (message broker), and virtual machines with pre-recorded data for easy deployment.
      - **Version Control**: Hosted on GitHub, implying version control via Git (though specifics like branching strategies or CI/CD pipelines are not detailed).
      - **Evolution Management**: No explicit mention of formal evolution strategies, but the adaptability of the twin suggests iterative updates based on sensor/environmental feedback.

    - **Air Quality Management**:
      - **Methodologies**: Model-Driven Digital Twin Engineering, focusing on indoor air quality simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization.
      - **Technologies**: Raspberry Pi with sensors for the physical system, AutomationML for the digital twin, and Microsoft Azure for pipeline implementation.
      - **Version Control**: Hosted on GitHub, implying Git-based versioning.
      - **Testing/Validation**: Three example applications (visualization, simulation, prediction) suggest iterative validation, though no explicit QA framework is described.
      - **Evolution Management**: No formal lifecycle governance mentioned, but the modular design (e.g., Azure tools) implies scalability for updates.

    - **Lab Scale Gantry Crane**:
      - **Methodologies**: Not explicitly detailed in the document, but the C4 Model is used for architecture visualization, suggesting structured design phases.
      - **Technologies**: Likely ad-hoc (similar to other exemplars), with no mention of frameworks or commercial tools.
      - **Version Control**: Not mentioned.
      - **Evolution Management**: No lifecycle governance or transition management described.

    **Common Observations**:
    - All exemplars use **containerization/virtualization** for dissemination but lack formalized lifecycle governance.
    - **Ad-hoc development** dominates, with no mention of standardized methodologies (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010 for DT lifecycle).
    - **No explicit transition management** between phases (e.g., from prototype to operational DT).

      - **Analytics**: AutomationML-based modeling for physics-based simulations; Azure likely supports advanced analytics.
      - **Decision Support**: Recommendations for ventilation/airflow adjustments (e.g., virus mitigation).

    - **Lab Scale Gantry Crane**:

    **Common Observations**:

extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **InfluxDB** for data storage, which inherently includes basic access control mechanisms (e.g., role-based permissions) and encryption for data at rest. However, explicit documentation on **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., TLS for data-in-transit, OAuth 2.0 for authentication) or **threat mitigation strategies** (e.g., intrusion detection systems, anomaly detection) is not provided. The system relies on **ActiveMQ** for message brokering, which supports **JMS security policies** but lacks detailed descriptions of **fail-safe mechanisms** or **risk assessment approaches** in the provided context.

The **Air Quality Management** exemplar utilizes **Raspberry Pi sensors** and **AutomationML-based digital twins**, with **Microsoft Azure** hosting the pipeline. Azure provides built-in **security controls** (e.g., Azure Active Directory for authentication, role-based access control, and encryption for data storage). However, the documents do not specify **data anonymization techniques**, **consent management frameworks**, or **data sharing agreements** for third-party access. The exemplar does not explicitly mention **safety-critical system considerations** (e.g., compliance with IEC 62443 for industrial cybersecurity) or **fail-safe protocols** for sensor failures or network disruptions.

The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system lacks explicit documentation on **security and safety measures** in the provided context. While the **C4 Model** is used for architecture visualization, no details are provided regarding **access control mechanisms**, **authentication systems**, or **threat mitigation strategies** (e.g., secure communication protocols, hardware-level security for IoT devices). Similarly, **risk assessment approaches** (e.g., FMEA for failure modes) or **safety-critical system considerations** (e.g., emergency stop protocols) are not addressed in the given documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
